title: Home plucking and gutting a chicken
subtitle: Wet plucking, dry plucking, evisceration, and cooling a home-raised bird
excerpt: Processing a home-raised bird is a practical skill with a clear sequence of steps. This guide covers both wet and dry plucking, the full evisceration process, and how to cool the carcass safely before refrigeration or the freezer.
sourceNotes: Harvey Ussery, The Small-Scale Poultry Flock (2011), chapter on slaughter and processing. River Cottage Handbook No. 4: Poultry (2009). UK Food Standards Agency, Safe Catering: Poultry handling guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Plucking feathers after the carcass has been dipped in water heated to 58 to 65 degrees Celsius. The hot water relaxes the feather follicles and makes removal much faster and easier than dry plucking.

Removing the internal organs from a slaughtered bird. The process separates edible offal (liver, heart, gizzard) from the digestive tract. Keeping the gut intact during removal prevents contaminating the carcass.

The water temperature used during wet plucking. Too cool and the feathers resist removal. Too hot and the skin tears or begins to cook. 60 to 62 degrees Celsius works for most breeds; heavier, older birds may need 63 to 65 degrees.

content.text: Processing your own birds takes roughly an hour for the whole sequence once you have done it a few times. The first time takes longer. Work methodically and keep a clean bucket of water nearby throughout.

attrs.title: Dispatch is a separate step
attrs.body: This guide covers processing after the bird has already been dispatched. Dispatch must be done correctly and lawfully before any of the steps below. In the UK, home slaughter for personal consumption is legal without a licence; slaughter for sale requires a licensed slaughter person and an approved premises.

content.text: Wet plucking vs dry plucking
Wet plucking

is faster and easier. It suits most home setups. You need a large bucket or tub and a thermometer. Dry plucking requires no equipment but takes three times as long and leaves more feather stubs. Dry plucking is sometimes preferred for birds destined for long hanging, as wet plucking softens the skin.
Preparing the scald water
Bring water to the correct
scalding temperature

of 60 to 62 degrees Celsius before the bird is dispatched. You want the water ready immediately after dispatch. Adding a drop of washing-up liquid breaks the surface tension and helps the water penetrate the feathers.
Scalding and plucking

Submerge the whole carcass in the scald water. Move it up and down to work water through the feathers. Sixty seconds is usually enough for a standard laying breed. Heavier meat birds may need 90 seconds.
Test a wing feather. Pull firmly. If it releases cleanly, the bird is ready. If it resists, return to the water for 15 seconds more.
Pluck in the direction of growth, not against it. Work the breast first, then the back, legs, and wings. Wing tips and the area around the oil gland at the tail base are the most difficult.
Use needle-nosed pliers to pull pin feathers. These are immature feathers that did not fully emerge. Grip at the base and pull firmly.
Removing the head and feet

Cut the head off at the base of the neck with a sharp, heavy knife or poultry shears. Remove the feet by cutting through the knee joint. Bend the joint backwards first to find the cartilage gap, then cut through it cleanly. Avoid sawing through bone.
Evisceration
Evisceration
is the step that most people find hardest at first. The goal is to remove everything inside the body cavity without breaking the gut, the bile duct, or the crop.
Make a small horizontal cut below the keel bone, just above the vent. Keep the cut shallow. You only need to break through the abdominal wall, not into the organs.
Widen the cut sideways with scissors until you have a gap wide enough to put a hand inside. Keep clear of the vent.
Reach in with a flat hand, palm up, fingers together. Loosen the organs from the cavity wall by pushing your hand around the inside edge in a sweeping motion.

Pull the organ mass out in one movement, keeping it whole. The gut and vent come out together. Set the mass on a board. Locate the gizzard, liver, and heart and set them aside. Discard the gut, lungs, and other organs.
Remove the crop from the neck end. The crop is a sac at the base of the neck filled with the bird's last meal. Loosen it with your fingers and pull it free.

attrs.title: If you break the gut
attrs.body: Stop immediately. Wash the cavity thoroughly under cold running water. A small breach is recoverable if you clean fast and well. Green bile from the gall bladder is harder to remove; trim any green-stained flesh completely. If the smell persists after washing, discard the carcass.

content.text: Cooling the carcass

Rinse the carcass inside and out under cold running water. Place it in a bowl of iced water or very cold water for at least one hour. The core temperature must drop below 4 degrees Celsius within four hours of slaughter. After chilling, dry the carcass, cover it, and refrigerate it. Rest for 24 hours in the fridge before cooking. This allows the muscles to relax and improves texture.

entries.cause: Water was too hot, the bird scalded for too long, or feathers were pulled against the grain
entries.fix: Torn skin does not affect eating quality. For the next bird, reduce scald time by 15 seconds and test a feather earlier. Pull with the grain of growth, not across it.

entries.cause: Gut was nicked during evisceration, or the bird was not cooled fast enough after slaughter
entries.fix: Rinse the cavity thoroughly with cold water. Trim any discoloured tissue. If the smell is strong throughout the carcass, do not eat it.

entries.cue: Many pin feathers remain after plucking
entries.cause: Bird was mid-moult at slaughter, or scald temperature was too low
entries.fix: Use needle-nosed pliers for pin feathers. Avoid processing birds that are actively moulting; the feather quality and skin condition will both be poor.
